judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with equivalent grounding; Report 2 is marginally more thorough (flags malformed-value handling and proposes AND-ing a master switch with per-tenant value) but the difference doesn't affect expectation coverage — the two are comparable.

report_1.evidence: Reminders **will be sent** automatically. The decision is made in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py`, line 11–12... if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:  # line 11\n return True   # line 12 — decides the unset case

report_2.evidence: Yes — reminders **will be sent automatically**. The deciding code is `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `backend/app/feature_flags.py`... if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:   # line 11\n return True    # line 12

report_1.evidence: Where I'd change it: `backend/app/feature_flags.py`, line 12 — return `False` instead of `True` for the unset case (or collapse the body to `return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))`, which yields `False` when absent).

report_2.evidence: I would change it. Invert the `None` branch in `backend/app/feature_flags.py:11–12` so an unset value returns `False` (e.g. `if not tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"): return False`...

report_1.evidence: Git history shows the `is None → return True` pattern was introduced in commit `25509a7` (SLOT-190) for `compact_calendar_view_enabled` — a calendar *display layout* preference, where defaulting on is harmless. ... The precedent it copied was justified for a harmless UI preference (worst case: a slightly different calendar view). Applying the same "absent means on" default to an action that dispatches real SMS/email to patients changes the stakes entirely

report_2.evidence: Commit `25509a7` (SLOT-190) introduced `compact_calendar_view_enabled` — a calendar *view* UI preference — using the `if ... is None: return True` pattern... The two flags are in different blast-radius categories: the calendar-view flag is a harmless UI preference where grandfather-on is fine, whereas the reminders flag triggers automated outbound SMS/email.

report_1.evidence: **Add a global gate / safety valve.** The batch `run()` in `reminders.py` has no global kill-switch, no environment guard, no dry-run, and no idempotency... `infra/main.tf` defines an `environment` variable (`"staging"`) but nothing wires it to the job, so there's no guard against blasting real messages from a non-prod run. I'd add a global enable flag and a dry-run/preview path before go-live.

report_2.evidence: **Add an env-level master switch and AND it with the per-tenant value.** Currently the per-clinic flag is the only gate (`reminders.py:25`). A master gate (off by default, ideally read as a strict literal like `== "true"`) gives one lever to keep the whole feature dark during rollout and one clear rollback affordance to flip it back off.
